name: teal paint
shortDescription: A pot of teal paint mixed for shutters, harbor signs, shrine trim, and fashionable rooms that want blue's calm without abandoning green's life. It sells especially well in coastal towns, where buyers insist the shade reminds them of cleaner water than they usually see.
flavorText: “Teal is what the sea looks like in a merchant's memory.”

name: white paint
shortDescription: A pot of white paint used for doors, walls, signs, holy marks, and the eternal struggle against soot, fingerprints, and time. Builders buy it by the bucket, while landlords buy it to make old rooms look briefly innocent again.
flavorText: “White paint forgives nothing; it merely delays confession.”

name: yellow paint
shortDescription: A bright yellow paint for signs, festival boards, wagon trim, toy stalls, and cheerful lies told to tired buildings. It catches light well, making it valuable to merchants who need passersby to notice a shop before noticing the prices.
flavorText: “Yellow paint is sunlight with a lid.”

name: Crowncards tournament trophy
shortDescription: A Crowncards tournament trophy polished for winners, collectors, and card halls that turn arguments into ceremony. It is less useful than coin but far better for bragging, especially when displayed beside a deck that may or may not have earned it honestly.
flavorText: “Every trophy says victory; only the table remembers how loudly.”

name: scarce enchanted tengu statue
shortDescription: A scarce enchanted tengu statue carved with watchful wings, sharp posture, and a faint charm that makes nearby air feel expectant. Scholars and shrine keepers debate its origin, while collectors simply enjoy owning something that seems to judge guests from across the room.
flavorText: “The beak is stone. The disapproval feels alive.”

name: large angel statue
shortDescription: A large angel statue with lifted wings, solemn features, and enough weight to make transport a devotional exercise. Temples, graveyards, estates, and wealthy gardens prize it for grace, protection, and the useful impression that someone holy is keeping watch.
flavorText: “Stone wings do not fly, but they make people lower their voices.”

name: archer statue
shortDescription: An archer statue posed with bow drawn and gaze fixed on a target only the sculptor could see. It suits gates, training yards, hunting lodges, and collectors who appreciate discipline caught at the exact moment before release.
flavorText: “The arrow never leaves, which is why the warning lasts.”

name: broken statue
shortDescription: A broken statue missing dignity in several visible places, valuable to repairers, ruin collectors, and scholars who prefer fragments because they argue less with theories. Its damage lowers the price, unless someone can prove the missing pieces belonged to someone famous.
flavorText: “A broken statue asks whether history fell or was pushed.”

name: small cat statue
shortDescription: A small cat statue with a neat pose, pointed ears, and the smug calm of an animal that expects offerings without explanation. It sells well to homes, temples, shop counters, and anyone hoping a carved cat brings fewer mice than a real one.
flavorText: “Stone cats are cheaper to feed and just as judgmental.”

name: large centaur statue
shortDescription: A large centaur statue showing horse strength and human pride joined in one difficult piece of stone. It commands space in courtyards, academies, and noble gardens, where buyers admire the craft and movers quietly curse the legs.
flavorText: “Two natures, four hooves, one very expensive delivery.”

name: sacrifice statue
shortDescription: A sacrifice statue carved with ritual posture and unsettling restraint, the kind of relic that makes priests speak carefully and collectors ask too many questions. Its value depends on age, provenance, and whether the buyer wants reverence or scandal.
flavorText: “Some stone remembers ceremonies best left abbreviated.”

name: fabled statue of the Springtide
shortDescription: A fabled statue of the Springtide, shaped around renewal, rising water, and the old belief that seasons can be invited if praised properly. Temples and collectors seek it fiercely, because fabled hope is still rare enough to price like treasure.
flavorText: “When the tide returns, even stone pretends to bloom.”

name: hero statue
shortDescription: A hero statue with lifted chin, proud stance, and enough idealized muscle to flatter anyone's ancestor. Town squares, barracks, and ambitious families buy such monuments to turn complicated lives into simple poses that children can point at.
flavorText: “Heroes are easiest to understand once carved silent.”

name: dragon statue
shortDescription: A dragon statue coiled in ornamental threat, all ridges, claws, and carefully permanent menace. It suits wealthy halls, wizard towers, and gateposts where the owner wants power implied without feeding anything larger than a mason's invoice.
flavorText: “Stone dragons guard treasure mostly by suggesting better choices.”

name: deepforge statue
shortDescription: A deepforge statue cut in heavy lines, honoring heat, labor, and the old craft-cults of furnace halls below the mountain roads. Smiths value its symbolism, while merchants value that anything associated with deep forges somehow weighs twice as much.
flavorText: “The forge cools. The pride does not.”

name: scarce elementalist statue
shortDescription: A scarce elementalist statue marked with balanced motifs of flame, water, stone, and air, as if the sculptor feared choosing sides. It draws buyers from mage schools, collectors' salons, and sanctums where decorative philosophy can be charged by the inch.
flavorText: “Four elements, one invoice, no consensus.”

name: small elephant statue
shortDescription: A small elephant statue with sturdy legs, broad ears, and a friendly weight in the hand. Traders sell it as a charm of memory, luck, patience, or distant lands, depending on what the buyer seems most willing to believe.
flavorText: “Tiny elephants carry large meanings conveniently.”

name: blind statue
shortDescription: A blind statue carved with covered or empty eyes, giving it an air of judgment turned inward. Courts, temples, and philosophers favor it for symbolism, while ordinary buyers admit it makes a hallway feel both wise and uncomfortable.
flavorText: “It sees nothing, which is why people confess near it.”

name: seeing statue
shortDescription: A seeing statue with open eyes and a posture designed to make passersby feel noticed. It is common in watch halls, shrines, and guarded estates, where symbolic vigilance costs less than another sentry and never asks for supper.
flavorText: “Stone eyes cannot blink, and that is their advantage.”

name: gold family idol statue
shortDescription: A golden family idol statue shaped for household devotion, inheritance rooms, and private rituals where memory and wealth are allowed to sit together. Its gold draws thieves, its meaning draws heirs, and its price draws arguments from both.
flavorText: “Family shines brightest when someone has polished the legacy.”

name: scarce dark ironclan statue
shortDescription: A scarce dark Ironclan statue, blackened and severe, carrying the hard-edged style of a people who respected endurance more than ornament. Collectors seek it for rarity, while smiths nod at the metalwork before pretending not to be impressed.
flavorText: “Ironclan art does not decorate a room. It occupies it.”

name: fabled firespitter statue
shortDescription: A fabled firespitter statue with a snarling mouth, charred legends, and enough old enchantment about it to make cautious owners keep it away from curtains. Whether it ever spat fire hardly matters; the rumor alone keeps its value hot.
flavorText: “Some flames die. Better stories smolder.”

name: granite bust
shortDescription: A granite bust showing head, shoulders, and the stern permanence that only hard stone can lend to a mortal face. It is popular with guild founders, minor nobles, and families who want ancestry heavy enough to discourage rearranging the room.
flavorText: “Granite makes even forgettable men difficult to move.”

name: fabled reflection statue
shortDescription: A fabled reflection statue polished and shaped to catch faces in ways that seem slightly more honest than glass. Mages, judges, and vain collectors all desire it, though for very different reasons and with equally nervous attendants.
flavorText: “It reflects the viewer, then waits for the viewer to notice.”

name: imp statue
shortDescription: An imp statue crouched in mischievous stone, with sharp ears, bent limbs, and a grin that makes owners blame it for missing buttons. It is popular as a garden oddity, tavern mascot, or warning that good taste has left unattended.
flavorText: “A stone imp cannot cause trouble, which is exactly what it wants you to think.”

name: scarce mable Stormmere the first statue
shortDescription: A scarce mable Stormmere the First statue, prized despite the cataloger's spelling and possibly because of it. The marble figure carries founding-age importance, making historians argue, collectors lean closer, and porters demand payment before touching the base.
flavorText: “Even misspelled marble can be politically heavy.”

name: mermaid statue
shortDescription: A mermaid statue carved with flowing hair, scaled tail, and the old coastal promise that beauty and danger share the same tide. It sells well in harbor towns, bathhouses, gardens, and inns hoping sailors spend coin before remembering home.
flavorText: “Mermaids are safer in stone and still expensive.”

name: reef serpent statue
shortDescription: A reef serpent statue twisting in carved coils, marked with fins, ridges, and the sleek threat of something made for coral shadows. Coastal collectors prize it, while fishers prefer it facing away from the door for reasons they call practical.
flavorText: “The reef keeps secrets; the statue keeps the shape of one.”

name: perfect obelisk statue
shortDescription: A perfect obelisk statue cut in clean lines, balanced faces, and a point so precise it makes masons either proud or jealous. Its power is in symmetry, giving gardens, halls, and ritual spaces an air of deliberate mystery.
flavorText: “Perfection is suspicious when it stands too straight.”

name: scarce statue of discovery
shortDescription: A scarce statue of discovery, posed with lifted gaze and open hand as if revealing a road just beyond sight. Explorers' guilds, schools, and ambitious patrons value it because curiosity looks nobler once carved at full height.
flavorText: “Discovery begins as a question and ends as a plaque.”

name: orb guardian statue
shortDescription: An orb guardian statue built around a protective stance and a central sphere that invites both reverence and theft. It appears in sanctums, vault gardens, and old towers where owners want visitors unsure whether the orb is decoration or warning.
flavorText: “Guardians need not move when curiosity does the work.”

name: orb statue
shortDescription: An orb statue formed around a smooth central sphere, simple enough to seem decorative and strange enough to start theories. Scholars see celestial order, fortune tellers see signs, and merchants see a round object that somehow sells as destiny.
flavorText: “A polished sphere lets everyone find their own omen.”

name: mable ironclan idol statue
shortDescription: A mable Ironclan idol statue, pale, heavy, and sternly shaped despite the questionable spelling in the ledger. It combines clan symbolism with ceremonial weight, making it desirable to collectors who forgive clerical errors faster than chipped stone.
flavorText: “The inscription may stumble; the idol does not.”

name: princess statue
shortDescription: A princess statue with graceful posture, careful gown work, and the polished calm expected of royalty that never has to answer questions. Gardens, courtyards, and sentimental patrons favor it because stone nobility behaves better than living heirs.
flavorText: “A stone princess never disputes succession.”

name: sigmund statue
shortDescription: A Sigmund statue carved with enough specificity to suggest the name once meant more than the seller can prove. It attracts local historians, family claimants, and bargain collectors willing to gamble that forgotten importance can become fashionable again.
flavorText: “Every named statue is famous somewhere, even if only to itself.”

name: copper snail statue
shortDescription: A copper snail statue with a curling shell, patient body, and warm metal glow that improves with careful polish or honest age. Gardeners, scholars, and eccentric collectors enjoy it because slowness looks wiser when made from copper.
flavorText: “Copper tarnishes faster than a snail hurries.”

name: scarce enchanted ghost statue
shortDescription: A scarce enchanted ghost statue shaped in pale stone and faint glamour, making its edges seem less certain than they should. It is prized for haunted halls, mage collections, and theatrical estates where atmosphere can be priced like silver.
flavorText: “It is hard to prove a statue moved when it already looks half absent.”

name: statue hydra
shortDescription: A statue hydra with multiple heads arranged in a knot of carved threat, each expression disagreeing with the others. It suits menageries, arena gates, and collectors of monster lore, though dusting it requires either patience or surrender.
flavorText: “One neck is sculpture. Several become a maintenance problem.”

name: snail statue
shortDescription: A snail statue of plain stone, simple, calm, and oddly reassuring in gardens or quiet courtyards. It is not grand art, but it gives small spaces a patient charm and lets owners claim they appreciate humble natural forms.
flavorText: “A snail in stone has finally reached its destination.”

name: swamp monster statue
shortDescription: A swamp monster statue hunched with reeds, claws, and a face carved to make sensible people avoid marshes after sunset. Taverns, frontier forts, and collectors buy it as warning, decoration, or proof that fear can be made marketable.
flavorText: “The swamp is worse, but this is easier to deliver.”

name: sword of Crownfall statue
shortDescription: A sword of Crownfall statue honoring a blade that may be history, symbol, or courtly exaggeration depending on who is paying. Its clean lines and royal association make it useful in halls where loyalty must look sharp and permanent.
flavorText: “A carved sword cannot cut, but it can still divide a room.”

name: tengu statue
shortDescription: A tengu statue with birdlike bearing, folded power, and the alert stillness of a mountain spirit pretending to be decoration. Shrines, collectors, and duel schools favor it, especially when placed where apprentices feel watched.
flavorText: “A tengu made of stone still looks ready to correct your stance.”

name: statue of reflection
shortDescription: A statue of reflection polished around face, water, or mirror motifs, inviting quiet thought before the buyer remembers how much it costs. It is popular in gardens, meditation rooms, and wealthy homes where introspection needs a centerpiece.
flavorText: “Reflection is cheaper in ponds, but stone lasts longer.”

name: twins statue
shortDescription: A twins statue showing two figures linked by pose, gaze, or shared base, depending on the sculptor's intended symbolism. Families, temples, and collectors read unity into it; merchants read the practical difficulty of packing two faces safely.
flavorText: “Two figures make one statue and twice the interpretation.”

name: vine monster statue
shortDescription: A vine monster statue tangled with carved tendrils, leaf claws, and the uneasy suggestion that gardens may hold grudges. It sells well to monster collectors, hedge mages, and estate owners who want their landscaping to feel armed.
flavorText: “When a garden wants a guard dog, it grows teeth.”

name: market warrior statue
shortDescription: A market warrior statue made for public squares, training yards, and merchants who sell courage in visible form. It is less noble than a hero monument but more practical, with a direct pose that tells customers exactly what kind of strength is for sale.
flavorText: “Public bravery often begins as a purchase order.”

name: fabled marble mermaid of Pearlgate statue
shortDescription: A fabled marble mermaid of Pearlgate statue, famous for pale stone, sea-born grace, and the coastal pride carved into every curve. Pearlgate collectors prize it fiercely, while inland buyers pay extra for the idea of owning a safer ocean.
flavorText: “Pearlgate claims the sea. This marble claims Pearlgate.”

name: hollow ghost statue
shortDescription: A hollow ghost statue shaped with empty spaces and thin stone folds that make it feel lighter than its weight permits. It suits haunted gardens, theatrical corridors, and collectors who enjoy art that seems to be missing itself on purpose.
flavorText: “The hollowness is not a flaw. It is where the shiver lives.”

name: scarce Maho statue
shortDescription: A scarce Maho statue with unfamiliar styling, careful facial detail, and enough regional mystery to make appraisers ask for better light. Its rarity draws collectors first, then scholars, then wealthy buyers who enjoy owning names other people cannot place.
flavorText: “Rarity begins where certainty ends.”

name: fabled statue of eternity
shortDescription: A fabled statue of eternity carved to suggest endless time, endless stillness, and the uncomfortable fact that stone is better at patience than people. Temples, rulers, and collectors seek it because permanence is easiest to admire when purchased.
flavorText: “Eternity is heavy, expensive, and never in a hurry.”
